# Questions in Livestream 133

Live Stream #133: The ETS D&D Special Live Stream

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=4620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:00</a> | What Greek textbook would he recommend? | He recommended William Mounce's introductory Greek textbook as a strong beginner and self-teaching option. | He called William Mounce's Greek textbook an excellent introductory option because it is accessible even for beginners with little language background. He emphasized that it works well for people trying to teach themselves Greek and described it as the best introductory Greek textbook available. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=4690" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:10</a> | Did Leviticus 18:22 originally condone the act and later get redacted because of a cultural shift? | No. He argued the verse is framed by idolatry material and there is no evidence that it once condoned the act and was later revised. | He rejected the redaction proposal, saying the verse is sandwiched between idolatry-related material and therefore cannot be treated as if it originally condoned the act. To argue that, someone would need evidence that the line was inserted from somewhere else, and he said there is no evidence sufficient to warrant that claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=5048" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:08</a> | Was Kiara Falk still doing producer work for the stream, or was he going solo? | She was still producing from the hotel setup, but device and Wi-Fi problems limited her normal online presence. | He said the producer was present and still reading comments and listening, but the hotel setup forced them to swap devices. His Chromebook did not work well with the hotel's Wi-Fi, so she gave him her laptop and used the Chromebook, which made it harder for her to appear online as usual. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=5190" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:30</a> | Did he attend the panel on Adam and Eve, and if so what were his thoughts? | He said he did not attend it because they arrived late the night before; he heard the time slot hurt attendance. | He did not attend because they reached the hotel around 10 p.m. after a long travel day and were too tired the next morning. He said IP attended and reported that attendance was lower than expected, not because of poor content but because it was scheduled first thing in the morning while many people were still arriving. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=5267" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:47</a> | Will he do live D&D again? | Probably not soon; he described this as a special one-off treat, though another future event was possible. | He said they probably would not do live D&D again in the near future, though maybe they could do it again the next year. He framed the event as a special one-off treat caused by being at ETS and unable to run the usual livestream setup, and he noted that the players did well for a first-level speed game. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=5352" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:12</a> | Why were the camera and connection worse than IP, David, and John's setup? | He was streaming from a hotel on an older laptop after the Chromebook did not work well with the hotel Wi-Fi. | He explained that the Chromebook had the better camera but did not work properly with the hotel's network, so they switched to an older laptop with better Wi-Fi just before the stream. Unlike IP, David, and John, they did not bring a dedicated mobile streaming setup, so they sacrificed camera quality for a more stable connection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=5447" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:47</a> | Were there any interesting ETS encounters, especially concerning members of AVR? | Yes. He described conversations with ETS attendees and scholars where the channel and prior debate clips had come up. | He told a story from an archaeological session moderated by Petrovich, where he thought Petrovich undermined students from another archaeological center during introductions. He also described asking a student presenter whether a seven-cup ritual object might be a perfumery item for temple smells, said the student found the suggestion useful, and noted other ABR-related observations such as the Bible Seminary table being placed inside another booth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=5796" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:36</a> | How was ETS overall? | He said it was a good and enjoyable conference, especially for meeting people and having in-person conversations. | He described ETS as fantastic, especially for meeting IP, David Wood, viewers, and old colleagues. He said the networking was stronger than the papers, mentioned useful conversations with a media group and a publisher about his plagues book, and said the conference was exhausting but very successful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=6165" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:45</a> | Did he get any new books besides the NET Bible? | Only a few. He mentioned buying a biblical archaeology book and seeing other unusual translations, but not buying many books. | He said they did not buy many books because the offerings were not especially interesting that year. He saw an odd Bible translation he did not buy, bought a biblical archaeology book to compare and evaluate as a possible primer, and noted that his own book sold out by the second day. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=6343" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:43</a> | What was his favorite session or talk at ETS? | He singled out David Yoon's paper on semantic and exegetical errors in annihilationism, with another ritual-cups paper also standing out. | His favorite paper was David Yoon's critique of semantic and exegetical errors in annihilationist arguments. He said Yoon steel-manned the arguments before taking them apart, had clearly done his homework, and kept the paper focused; he also liked the ritual-cups paper, though it left the issue less resolved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=6541" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:01</a> | In clan genealogy, are there ever disputes over who counts as the greatest member? | Yes. He said succession disputes and rival claims can happen, sometimes even splitting clans into new branches. | He said disputes can happen when leadership or succession is unclear, such as when an older brother has a claim but a younger brother is stronger or more accomplished. These disputes can produce fights, feuds, and sometimes clan splits, though he stressed that this was not constant and only occurred often enough to be recognized. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=6647" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:47</a> | Were there any ETS talks he thought were just bad? | Yes. He singled out one paper on the Exodus as badly argued and said it mishandled both the text and Egyptological context. | He singled out a paper arguing that the Exodus account was essentially a polemic against Baal, using the geography of the route to make the case. He criticized it as textually and Egyptologically weak, saying the presenter mishandled Egyptian conceptions of gods, Baal-zephon, and the sea-god argument, and that the overall analysis of Egyptian religion was poor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=6903" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:03</a> | Was Andrew Loke's The Origin of Humanity and Evolution available at ETS? | He did not see it and was not looking for it; after the follow-up, he noted T&T Clark was not at ETS that year. | He did not see the book, but said he also was not looking for it and would have needed to know the publisher. After a follow-up identified T&T Clark as the publisher, he said that explained it because T&T Clark was not at ETS that year, and not every publisher attends ETS. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=6996" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:36</a> | What is the difference between an unclear Egyptian term and the ib or heart? | The transcript does not preserve the first term clearly. He said he would need to look that up, but described the ib as the heart, mind, consciousness, or internal essence shown in the weighing of the heart scene. | The first term is unclear in the transcript, and he said he would have to look it up because it was not coming to him off the top of his head. For the ib, he described it as the person's heart or mind, the consciousness and internal essence represented in scenes such as the weighing of the heart. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=7252" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:52</a> | Had he taken a selfie with David Wood? | No. David Wood was rushing out as he arrived, but he did get a selfie with IP and Mr. EC. | He said he did not get a selfie with David Wood because Wood was rushing out of the convention center while he was coming in. He did get a selfie with IP and Mr. EC and said they hoped to post that photo soon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=7417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:37</a> | How did his meeting with David Wood go? | It was very brief; David Wood was leaving while he was entering, and IP still hoped to facilitate a fuller meeting later. | He said the meeting was extremely brief, with Wood walking out while he was walking in, and he got the impression Wood may not have realized who he was. He did not press the interaction, thought Wood seemed distracted because he was rushing out, and said IP still planned to get them together later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=7647" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:27</a> | Who is Pharaoh Sesostris? | He explained that Sesostris is a Greek form for the Senusret kings of Dynasty 12 in the Middle Kingdom. | He explained that Sesostris is the Greek form of Senusret, referring to three Dynasty 12 Middle Kingdom kings. He described them as extremely powerful rulers who represent the peak or height of the Middle Kingdom, while noting that Egyptologists today generally use Senusret rather than the Greek form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=8800" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:40</a> | Did the earlier David Falk and Titus Kennedy debate come up at ETS? | Yes. He said students and scholars had been discussing clips from the debate, including the Amorites/Ammonites exchange. | He said Steve Ortiz told him that students were discussing the channel and especially the debate with Titus Kennedy, including the clip about Ammonites versus Amorites. Falk took this as evidence that the debate had been circulating in academic and seminary contexts and that students were using clips to analyze what went wrong or right. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m0lfwar6BD0?t=9091" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:31</a> | Was there anything amusing or fun at ETS that he wanted to talk about? | He described enjoyable viewer meetings, Mr. EC photo moments, and a funny exchange where Steve Ortiz knew him before recognizing Michael Jones. | He said meeting viewers was a highlight, including people wanting selfies with Mr. EC and stopping him in the halls. He also described a funny moment where Steve Ortiz spoke as if he knew Falk's work, then turned to Michael Jones and did not recognize him at first, which Falk found amusing given Michael's larger YouTube presence. |
